twitter_thread: 63% of households can't afford a $1k emergency. I cut my expenses by $800/mo using a zero-based budget.
Automating savings with ifttt and bank APIs added $540 to my safety net in the first year.
Paying off my $10k credit card debt at 22% interest saved me $2,200/yr in interest.
Using the 50/30/20 rule for budgeting helped me allocate 30% of my income towards savings and debt repayment.
Not accounting for my $1,200/yr car insurance premium almost derailed my budget. Don't make the same mistake.
Prioritizing needs over wants helped me reduce dining out expenses from $800 to $200/mo.
What's the one expense you've cut that's had the biggest impact on your finances? #affordabilitycrisis #personalfinance
pack.linkedin: The affordability crisis affects 63% of households, who can't afford a $1k emergency. I've found that automating savings and paying off high-interest debt can be effective strategies. For example, paying off a $10k credit card debt at 22% interest can save $2,200/yr in interest. Using the 50/30/20 rule for budgeting and prioritizing needs over wants can also help. What concrete steps have you taken to address the affordability crisis in your own life or business?
tiktok_hooks: I was part of the 63% who couldn't afford a $1k emergency, until I made these three changes.
Want to know the one budgeting rule that saved me $1,000/mo?
High interest debt cost me $2,200/yr, until I paid off my $10k credit card balance.
pack.reddit_headline: Is the Affordability Crisis a Personal Finance Problem or a Systemic Issue?
